role_summary: First cousin and lifelong friend of Emmett Till (catalog external figure); the last surviving eyewitness to Till's August 24, 1955 visit to Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market in Money, Mississippi, and to Till's August 28, 1955 abduction from Moses Wright's home four nights later; pastor of Argo Temple Church of God in Christ in Argo, Illinois (over six decades); co-founder of the Emmett Till and Mamie Till-Mobley Memorial Foundation.
ai_reading: Parker's top semantic neighbor is Simeon Wright (entry 138) at a high cosine count of 247, a strong single-neighbor signature in this catalog and the catalog's clearest fellow-witness vector pairing: Wright is also Till's cousin, was also present at the August 28, 1955 Money, Mississippi events, and his oral history rhymes with Parker's testimony as the catalog's most-mutually-corroborating pair. The novel observation his page surfaces is the longitudinal-witness signature: Parker has been telling the Till story for seventy years (1955 to the present), and the embedding catches the stable narrative-form his testimony has taken across the multi-decade arc of being the primary surviving eyewitness; the Wright cosine reads as the same narrative-stability anchor in another voice rather than as fact-corroboration that historiography would predict.
biographical_paragraph: While Parker's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), his external biographical record is well-documented in his 2023 memoir [src: 1]. Parker was born August 16, 1939 in Schlater, Mississippi, the son of Wheeler Parker Sr. and Hallie Spearman Parker; his maternal grandfather Moses Wright was the great-uncle who Emmett Till was visiting in Money, Mississippi in August 1955. Parker, then fifteen, traveled to Mississippi with his cousin Emmett Till on the train from Chicago in late August 1955. He was present at Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market on August 24, 1955, when the contested encounter between Till and Carolyn Bryant occurred, and at Moses Wright's home in the early morning of August 28, 1955, when Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam took Till from his bed. After the murder Parker returned to Argo, Illinois and lived there for the rest of his life. In 1973 he was ordained in the Church of God in Christ and served as pastor of Argo Temple Church of God in Christ in Argo, Illinois for over six decades. His 2023 memoir A Few Days Full of Trouble: Revelations on the Journey to Justice for My Cousin and Best Friend, Emmett Till (with Christopher Benson, One World/Random House) is the canonical scholarly source on his life and his seventy-year role as the primary surviving witness to the events that catalyzed the modern civil rights movement [src: 2].

sources.title: Wheeler Parker, Jr. oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Wheeler Parker Jr. with Christopher Benson, A Few Days Full of Trouble: Revelations on the Journey to Justice for My Cousin and Best Friend, Emmett Till
